mission.title: Install and Configure Bfe on Kubernetes
mission.description: BFE (Beyond Front End) is a modern layer 7 load balancer developed by Baidu, designed to manage and route traffic efficiently. Installing BFE allows you to leverage its capabilities for advanced traffic management and load balancing in your Kubernetes environment.

steps.title: Add the BFE Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official BFE Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration. This will allow you to install BFE using Helm.
```bash
helm repo add bfe https://bfenetworks.github.io/bfe-helm-chart
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install BFE using Helm
steps.description:
Install BFE with a specific version in a new namespace called 'bfe'. This command will deploy the BFE server and its components.
```bash
helm install bfe bfe/bfe --version 1.8.1 --namespace bfe --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check the status of the BFE pods to ensure they are running correctly.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace bfe
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Edit the BFE deployment to set resource limits for better performance and stability.
```bash
kubectl edit deployment bfe --namespace bfe
# Add the following under spec.containers:
# resources:
#   limits:
#     cpu: "500m"
#     memory: "512Mi"
#   requests:
#     cpu: "250m"
#     memory: "256Mi"
```

steps.title: Set up port forwarding for access
steps.description:
Forward a local port to the BFE service to access it from your local machine.
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/bfe --namespace bfe 8080:80
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation will have the BFE pods running in the 'bfe' namespace, and you will be able to access the BFE service through port 8080 on your local machine. You should also see the configured resource limits applied to the BFE deployment.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add bfe https://bfenetworks.github.io/bfe-helm-chart
helm repo update

kubectl edit deployment bfe --namespace bfe
# Add the following under spec.containers:
# resources:
#   limits:
#     cpu: "500m"
#     memory: "512Mi"
#   requests:
#     cpu: "250m"
#     memory: "256Mi"

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Use the Helm command to uninstall the BFE release from the Kubernetes cluster. This will remove the deployment and associated resources managed by Helm.
```bash
helm uninstall bfe --namespace bfe
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
If there are any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) or persistent volume claims (PVCs) created by BFE, clean them up using the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd <crd-name>
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace bfe
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'bfe' namespace to remove all remaining resources associated with the BFE installation. Verify that the namespace has been deleted successfully.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace bfe
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's important to back up the current configuration and state of the BFE deployment. You can do this by exporting the current configuration:
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace bfe -o yaml > bfe-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository to ensure you have the latest charts and then upgrade the BFE installation to the desired version. Replace `<new-version>` with the version you want to upgrade to.
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade bfe bfe/bfe --version <new-version> --namespace bfe
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, check the status of the BFE pods to ensure they are running the new version correctly.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace bfe
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the BFE pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for the pods to diagnose the issue:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace bfe
```

troubleshooting.title: Service not accessible
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the BFE service, ensure that the port forwarding is set up correctly and that the service is running:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace bfe
kubectl port-forward svc/bfe --namespace bfe 8080:80
```

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources error
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter errors related to insufficient resources, check the resource limits and requests set in the deployment:
```bash
kubectl describe deployment bfe --namespace bfe
```

troubleshooting.title: Helm release not found
troubleshooting.description:
If you receive an error stating that the Helm release is not found, verify that you are using the correct namespace and release name:
```bash
helm list --namespace bfe
```

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, along with Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
